title: Semi-Analytical Modeling of Transient Stream Drawdown and Depletion in Response to Aquifer Pumping

research.question: How do stream drawdown and stream depletion evolve when a hydraulically connected stream has finite channel storage rather than a fixed stage?
research.system: A homogeneous anisotropic confined aquifer pumped near either a non-penetrating stream or a fully penetrating stream, with streambed conductance and finite stream-channel storage.
research.inputData: Analytical stream-aquifer geometry, six 2022 irrigation pumping events at Stenner Creek near Cal Poly San Luis Obispo, aquifer observation-well levels, and 15-minute stream-stage records from four stilling wells.
research.method: The study couples confined-aquifer flow and streambed leakage to a stream-channel mass balance, solves NPS and FPS cases in Laplace-Fourier space, derives stream depletion by integrating leakage, and estimates hydraulic and channel-storage parameters from observed drawdown.
research.benchmark: The semi-analytical solutions recover Theis or Ferris impermeable-boundary behavior as channel storage vanishes and Fox or Hantush fixed-stage behavior as channel storage becomes infinite; supporting information compares the solutions with a finite-element model.
research.uncertainty: Parameter standard errors come from the residual-variance-scaled inverse sensitivity covariance matrix; the field fit fixes selected parameters to reduce overparameterization and excludes stream routing, velocity, streambed storage, and vertical aquifer flow.
research.conclusion: Finite stream storage produces delayed but measurable stream drawdown, three-phase aquifer drawdown, and a depletion rate that peaks then declines; fixed-stage models can overestimate the stream contribution available during prolonged pumping.

narrative.throughline: A stream is not an infinite reservoir: once pumping draws down its finite channel storage, both stream stage and depletion depart from fixed-stage theory.
narrative.audienceStartingPoint: The audience understands that a pumping well lowers groundwater head and can capture water from a connected stream.
narrative.stakes: Assuming fixed stream stage can overstate groundwater supply and miss delayed stream decline or drying during sustained abstraction.
narrative.resolution: A mass balance turns channel storage into a measurable dynamic state, linking aquifer drawdown, delayed stream drawdown, and a finite stream-depletion pulse.

background.premise: Groundwater pumping near a connected stream can reduce streamflow enough to impair water use and ecosystem function.
background.visibleEvidence: A pumping cone intercepts the stream-aquifer connection while the stream hydrograph falls toward a dry channel.
background.whyNow: The management consequence must be visible before introducing the boundary condition that controls predicted depletion.
background.boundary: The paper studies pumping-induced exchange in a confined aquifer and does not model watershed-scale routing.
background.sourceExcerpt: reduced stream ﬂows, with undesirable impacts on both human use
background.visualObject: Stream cross-section above a pumping cone with declining discharge
background.motionPurpose: Draw water from the well until the hydraulic gradient redirects stream water toward the aquifer.
background.stepDetail: Show the initial stream and aquifer; start constant pumping; expand the cone; reduce the downstream discharge arrow.
background.transitionBridge: The falling stream arrow becomes a boundary-condition question.

background.premise: Existing fixed-stage analytical models force aquifer drawdown toward steady behavior even though observed near-stream drawdown can remain transient.
background.visibleEvidence: A fixed stream-stage line pins one model curve while field-like aquifer drawdown resumes rising at late time.
background.whyNow: The viewer needs to see the mismatch that motivates finite channel storage rather than another fixed-head solution.
background.boundary: The deficiency applies to the fixed-stage stream-depletion models reviewed in the paper.
background.sourceExcerpt: the cases of monotonically increasing with time before attaining a
background.visualObject: Fixed-stage boundary beside contrasting steady and late-rising drawdown curves
background.motionPurpose: Hold stream stage fixed in one curve and release it in the observed curve to reveal the missing state variable.
background.stepDetail: Plot early aquifer response; pin stream stage; show the fixed-stage plateau; overlay continued late-time drawdown.
background.transitionBridge: The pinned stream line becomes a finite water-storage column.

background.premise: Observed stream drawdown begins later than aquifer drawdown.
background.visibleEvidence: Aquifer drawdown begins first, followed more than an hour later by the stream-stage responses.
background.whyNow: The measured lag supplies the concrete phenomenon the model must reproduce.
background.boundary: The field evidence comes from six irrigation pumping events at one minimally penetrating stream-aquifer site.
background.locator: PDF page 13, Discussion, Drawdown Data Analysis
background.sourceExcerpt: drawdown responses are delayed relative to aquifer
background.visualObject: Aligned aquifer and stream drawdown traces with a visible onset lag
background.motionPurpose: Start the aquifer response first and delay the stream traces so the temporal ordering is unmistakable.
background.stepDetail: Mark pumping onset; reveal aquifer decline; hold the stream traces until their later measured onset.
background.transitionBridge: The lag interval becomes the storage-mediated stream response in the equations.

methods.input: Confined-aquifer geometry with a constant-rate line-sink well
methods.output: Two-dimensional anisotropic drawdown equation with a stream source-sink term
methods.validity: The aquifer is assumed homogeneous, confined, horizontally anisotropic, uniformly thick, and laterally infinite away from the stream.
methods.locator: PDF page 3, Methods, Governing Equations of Flow, Equation 1
methods.sourceExcerpt: The governing equation of the two-dimensional
methods.visualObject: Plan-view pumping sink embedded in an anisotropic aquifer field
methods.motionPurpose: Turn the expanding drawdown cone into directional curvature and storage terms.
methods.stepDetail: Place the well; orient x across the stream and y along it; stretch the cone by anisotropy; attach the source-sink strip.
methods.transitionBridge: The source-sink strip touches the streambed conductance layer.

methods.input: Aquifer drawdown and stream drawdown across a semipervious streambed
methods.validity: The Robin-type relation makes transfer proportional to the drawdown difference through a streambed of uniform conductance.
methods.locator: PDF page 3, Methods, Non-Penetrating Stream, Equation 3; PDF page 4, Equations 6-7
methods.sourceExcerpt: ∂s ∂2s ∂2s The stream–aquifer mass-transfer function, , at the
methods.visualObject: Vertical leakage arrows crossing a conductance layer between stream and aquifer heads
methods.motionPurpose: Scale the exchange arrows by the visible head difference across the streambed.
methods.stepDetail: Mark stream head; mark aquifer head; draw their difference; pass proportional arrows through the conductance layer.
methods.transitionBridge: The leakage arrows enter the finite stream water column.

methods.input: Streambed transfer flux entering or leaving the channel
methods.output: Time-dependent stream drawdown governed by finite channel storage
methods.validity: The rate of channel-water mass change equals pumping-induced transfer across the streambed when stream velocity is neglected.
methods.locator: PDF page 4, Methods, Accounting for Stream Drawdown and Channel Storage, Equations 8-9
methods.sourceExcerpt: change of mass within the stream channel equals the rate
methods.visualObject: Finite stream-storage column shrinking under streambed leakage
methods.motionPurpose: Convert exchange flux into a declining stream-stage state rather than an infinite fixed reservoir.
methods.stepDetail: Define the channel storage volume; admit the leakage flux; reduce stored volume; lower stream stage through time.
methods.transitionBridge: The coupled aquifer-stream geometry receives dimensionless scales.

methods.output: Dimensionless equations controlled by stream conductance and channel-storage ratios
methods.validity: The well-stream distance sets the characteristic length and hydraulic diffusivity sets the characteristic time.
methods.locator: PDF page 5, Methods, Analytical Solutions of the Flow Problem, Table 2
methods.sourceExcerpt: dimensionless variables and parameters that appear in the solution are deﬁned in Table 2.
methods.visualObject: Physical distances, time, head, and flux collapsing onto unit scales
methods.motionPurpose: Rescale the geometry so NPS and FPS responses can be compared with a small parameter set.
methods.stepDetail: Divide length by well-stream distance; divide time by diffusion time; scale head and flux; reveal the dimensionless groups.
methods.transitionBridge: The dimensionless field unfolds into transform coordinates.

methods.input: Dimensionless NPS and FPS equations in space and time
methods.output: Laplace-domain equations retaining cross-stream and along-stream coordinates
methods.validity: The homogeneous initial condition and linear dimensionless governing equations support the stated Laplace transform.
methods.locator: PDF page 5, Methods, Analytical Solutions of the Flow Problem, second paragraph, first transform statement
methods.sourceExcerpt: Laplace and Fourier-cosine transforms are applied to the
methods.visualObject: Dimensionless drawdown surface collapsing along a Laplace time axis
methods.motionPurpose: Replace time evolution with one Laplace-domain parameter while preserving both spatial coordinates.
methods.stepDetail: Hold both spatial coordinates fixed; map the time history to the Laplace variable; retain the transformed field.
methods.transitionBridge: The Laplace-domain field exposes the remaining along-stream coordinate.

methods.input: Laplace-domain equations retaining cross-stream and along-stream coordinates
methods.output: Ordinary differential equations in cross-stream position indexed by Laplace and Fourier-cosine variables
methods.validity: The source identifies the NPS solution as a Fourier cosine transform in the along-stream coordinate.
methods.locator: PDF page 5, Methods, Case of a Non-Penetrating Stream, Equation 10 and transform-variable definitions
methods.sourceExcerpt: cosine (tilde) transform of sD for the NPS case
methods.visualObject: Laplace-domain field unfolding into Fourier-cosine bands along the stream
methods.motionPurpose: Replace along-stream variation with a cosine spectral coordinate while retaining cross-stream position.
methods.stepDetail: Hold the Laplace variable fixed; map the along-stream profile to the cosine variable; expose the cross-stream ordinary differential equations.
methods.transitionBridge: The solved cross-stream spectra are ready for numerical inversion.

methods.input: Laplace-Fourier solutions for NPS or FPS aquifer and stream drawdown
methods.output: Laplace-domain aquifer and stream drawdown resolved along the stream
methods.validity: Numerical quadrature evaluates the Fourier-cosine inversion used by the semi-analytical implementation.
methods.locator: PDF page 6, Methods, Case of a Non-Penetrating Stream, paragraph following Equation 11, numerical-inversion sentence
methods.sourceExcerpt: inversion of the Fourier cosine and Laplace transforms using numerical
methods.visualObject: Fourier-cosine spectra resolving into Laplace-domain profiles along the stream
methods.motionPurpose: Integrate spectral contributions into a visible along-stream profile without changing the Laplace variable.
methods.stepDetail: Hold the Laplace variable fixed; evaluate the cosine inversion by numerical quadrature; reveal the along-stream profile.
methods.transitionBridge: The reconstructed spatial profile still carries the Laplace time parameter.

methods.input: Laplace-domain aquifer and stream drawdown resolved along the stream
methods.output: Physical-time aquifer and stream drawdown at each spatial position
methods.validity: The Stehfest algorithm performs the numerical Laplace inversion implemented in Mathematica and MATLAB.
methods.locator: PDF page 6, Methods, Case of a Non-Penetrating Stream, paragraph following Equation 11, Stehfest implementation clause
methods.visualObject: Laplace-domain profiles returning as aquifer and stream drawdown through time
methods.motionPurpose: Recover physical time at each spatial position with the stated numerical inversion algorithm.
methods.stepDetail: Hold each reconstructed spatial position fixed; apply the Stehfest weights; reveal its physical-time response.
methods.transitionBridge: The physical-time response family is checked against its limiting cases in M09.

methods.input: Physical-time NPS and FPS drawdown solutions across finite channel-storage values
methods.output: Finite-storage responses bracketed by impermeable-barrier and fixed-stage solutions
methods.validity: As dimensionless channel storage approaches zero the models recover Theis or Ferris behavior, while infinite storage recovers Fox or Hantush behavior.
methods.locator: PDF page 9, Results, Model Predicted Behavior, Figure 4
methods.sourceExcerpt: The limiting cases are included for
methods.visualObject: Finite-storage drawdown curves between zero-storage and infinite-storage limits
methods.motionPurpose: Show that the finite-storage response connects the established barrier and fixed-stage limits.
methods.stepDetail: Overlay the finite-storage curves with the zero-storage and infinite-storage limiting solutions.
methods.transitionBridge: The finite-storage response supplies the streambed flux integrated in M10.

methods.output: Stream-depletion rate normalized by pumping rate
methods.validity: The source integrates the streambed exchange flux along the stream to obtain total depletion.
methods.locator: PDF page 6, Methods, Stream Depletion Solution, Equations (17)-(19)
methods.sourceExcerpt: obtained by integrating the streambed flux along
methods.visualObject: Streambed exchange arrows accumulating into one depletion-rate gauge
methods.motionPurpose: Convert distributed streambed exchange into the reported total depletion response.
methods.stepDetail: Integrate exchange flux along the stream coordinate; normalize the total by pumping rate.
methods.transitionBridge: Observed aquifer and stream records are processed for field fitting.

methods.input: Raw observed aquifer and stream-stage time series
methods.validity: SSA is used to remove high-frequency and diurnal noise before pumping-response estimation.
methods.locator: PDF page 8, Application to Field Observations, Drawdown Analysis and Parameter Estimation
methods.sourceExcerpt: stream stage time series data using singular spectrum
methods.visualObject: Raw water-level trace decomposed into retained and discarded components
methods.motionPurpose: Expose the denoising operation separately from detrending.
methods.stepDetail: Decompose the raw series; discard noise components; retain the denoised trace.
methods.transitionBridge: Low-frequency variation is removed in M12.

methods.validity: EEMD removes the remaining trend and non-pumping variation identified by the field workflow.
methods.locator: PDF page 8, Application to Field Observations, Drawdown Analysis and Parameter Estimation
methods.visualObject: Denoised trace decomposed into trend and pumping-response components
methods.motionPurpose: Separate detrending from the preceding denoising step.
methods.stepDetail: Decompose the denoised series; remove the trend component; retain pumping-associated drawdown.
methods.transitionBridge: The extracted drawdown enters parameter fitting in M13.

methods.input: Observed aquifer or stream drawdown and the NPS solution
methods.output: Estimated conductivity, specific storage, conductance, and channel-storage coefficient
methods.validity: Levenberg-Marquardt minimization uses log-transformed positive parameters and the sum of squared model-observation residuals.
methods.locator: PDF page 8, Drawdown Analysis and Parameter Estimation, Equation 20
methods.sourceExcerpt: to identify parameter values that minimize the sum of
methods.visualObject: NPS curve bending toward aquifer and stream drawdown points as parameter dials settle
methods.motionPurpose: Move one model curve through the data while exposing which physical parameters control the fit.
methods.stepDetail: Initialize positive parameters; calculate model drawdown; form residuals; update parameters until the objective meets the criterion.
methods.transitionBridge: The fitted residual Jacobian enters covariance estimation.

methods.input: Residual Jacobian and fitted residual variance
methods.validity: Equation (21) uses the residual sensitivity matrix and residual variance under the local linear approximation.
methods.locator: PDF page 8, Drawdown Analysis and Parameter Estimation, Equation (21)
methods.sourceExcerpt: parameter estimates, which is computed as
methods.visualObject: Residual-sensitivity matrix becoming a parameter covariance matrix
methods.motionPurpose: Show covariance formation before extracting marginal errors.
methods.stepDetail: Combine the fitted residual sensitivity and variance terms according to Equation 21; retain V.
methods.transitionBridge: The diagonal variances are extracted in M15.

methods.output: Marginal variance for each fitted parameter
methods.validity: Only the covariance diagonal is used for the reported standard errors.
methods.locator: PDF page 8, Drawdown Analysis and Parameter Estimation, paragraph introducing Equation (21)
methods.sourceExcerpt: diagonal elements of the covariance matrix
methods.visualObject: Covariance matrix with its diagonal cells illuminated
methods.motionPurpose: Separate marginal variance extraction from covariance construction.
methods.stepDetail: Hold the covariance matrix; select each diagonal element; retain the marginal variances.
methods.transitionBridge: Square roots convert variances to standard errors in M16.

methods.output: Standard error for each fitted hydraulic parameter
methods.validity: The reported errors quantify local parameter-estimation uncertainty conditional on the fitted model.
methods.sourceExcerpt: respective standard errors for each parameter estimate are included
methods.visualObject: Variance bars transformed into standard-error whiskers
methods.motionPurpose: Complete the uncertainty calculation without implying posterior intervals.
methods.stepDetail: Take the square root of each marginal variance; attach the resulting standard error to its fitted parameter.
methods.transitionBridge: Return to the field fit with its conditional error bars.

symbols.visualObject: Constant pumping arrow at the well
symbols.transition: The well discharge arrow receives the label Q.
The falling aquifer head is bracketed and labeled s.
The vertical decline of channel stage receives the label s_r.
The channel water volume per unit stage change is labeled C_r.

symbols.meaning: Stream depletion rate as a fraction of pumping rate
symbols.transition: The accumulated stream flux is divided by the well arrow Q and labeled Q_r/Q.

return.visualObject: Stenner Creek and the pumped confined aquifer with measured aquifer drawdown, delayed stream drawdown, and a depletion hydrograph that peaks then declines
return.claim: Finite channel storage makes stream stage a transient state and limits the duration and magnitude of stream support to a pumping well.
return.claimBoundary: The demonstrated model neglects stream routing and velocity, streambed storage, and vertical aquifer flow; field parameter estimates are site- and test-specific, and aquifer-only fits did not reproduce stream drawdown without separate or joint analysis.
return.locator: PDF page 1, Abstract; PDF page 14, Conclusions and preceding Discussion
return.sourceExcerpt: stream depletion with transient stream drawdown is developed based on stream channel mass conservation and ﬁnite stream channel storage.

visual.primaryObject: Pumped confined aquifer hydraulically connected to a finite-storage stream
visual.accentObject: Delayed stream-stage trace and peaked stream-depletion hydrograph
visual.measuredQuantity: Aquifer drawdown, stream drawdown, and stream depletion rate
visual.geometryNotes: Maintain the stream, streambed, and pumping well in one cross-section; distinguish NPS and FPS geometries without morphing them together; use a shrinking channel-storage column to drive delayed stage decline and accumulate streambed flux into the depletion hydrograph.
